I'm mixed at the time. Teenagers were really popular. Mutants were really popular. And ninjas were really popular. So I just took all those and mixed them up with turtles. There you go. That's basically probably a billion dollars cents. So you can do the same thing in copy. What are two popular ideas or two things that are out there that we know that mark up responding and how to put them together? I'll give an example from last year. We know Jim Rickards is very hot as an individual. Like he's a lot of appeal. Now I don't mean like that. Our readers just, anything that guy says that they're interested in. We know gold was getting popular again, maybe based on the election or just the price of gold was going up. So combining Jim Rickards and gold ended up being an 11 million dollar idea. So if you look at the batch report, look at promos that are hot right now, what are the parts of those that you can combine with other parts to make something brand new? If it's a certain kind of technology, how do you match that up with another thing? For example, like 3D printing was hot and cancer was cured for hot, then combine them together. What's the 3D printing cured for cancer? So that's called the Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles technique. Another one is called the story nobody else is telling technique. Basically you try to find, fill in the blank, the blank story nobody else is telling in the marketplace right now. So what is the gold story that nobody else is telling right now? What is the Jim Rickards story that no one's told yet? That's going to be tough. There's a lot out there. But that's where the opportunity is. With no marijuana investing promos, or at least on the front end, seemed to have a lot of reader appeal. So what is the marijuana investor story nobody else is telling right now? So think of all the things that are working right now and just figure out what hasn't been told yet by specifically us or our competitors in the marketplace. It doesn't necessarily be like a story nobody has ever said in history or anything like that. But definitely what it means is nobody in our industry is talking about it right now. No one has put those things together. An easy way, the trick to that too, right, is that the blank needs to be something that's on top of mind for people. So when the election was rolling around, what's the election story no one's telling? If gold is top of mind because it's starting to form an uptrend, what's the gold story no one's telling? The easy way to know what's top of mind is coming back to the drudge. If you see something on the drudge report that's the headline for two weeks, it's going to be top of mind for our readers because these are the new sources that our readers are reading. So we have something coming up in what is going to be top of mind in eight days from now. I think it's eight days. I think it's inauguration. It's the inauguration story no one's telling right now. Like that could be a back end that's super hot, right? All of it in his prom of doing the Trump story no one's telling or the Trump combined with. And it's going to be, and that's where it gets tricky in our business because right now we should be putting out the Trump stories that no one, but as more and more as we do it and as our competitors do it, it becomes harder and harder, kind of like Jim Rickards where it's like, what is the story? Because a lot of them are being told. But sometimes it's going to be easy money too where everyone just assumes that everyone else is going to talk about Trump's investings. No one puts a promo out and then we do. I'll give you another calendar based event that's going to be hot and I'm going to ask Doug the question, the quiz. In three months, three months and three days, what's going to be hot? What's going to be top of mind? Taxes. You guys got three months and three days to write that promo. What's the tax story no one's telling? How do you save the most money on taxes this season? You know what's coming up? You've got three months. You can be ahead of it right now. What changed? What laws changed? Yeah. Oxford Club's got another promo out there that I guarantee you'll see heat up in the next couple of weeks. It's the same one that we talked about, right, the other day. I think you said you read, which I call the free money from Obama program. False aside again. I'll put my bias aside about what I feel in that promo. But it's about stimulus checks around tax time. I guarantee they start running that promo. I guarantee you will see the Oxford Club start to heat up on the web batch. So what's our tax story that no one's telling? Doug's got a publication that could fulfill it in Lausse Faire. He's got some tax contributors. What's the law change? What's the new unique loophole? It's probably less hot now that Trump is in there than what it would have been if Clinton were in there. But it's still going to be top of mind. Everybody that has a dollar of income pretty much got to fill out tax return, right, in three months. Everybody needs it. It's going to be very top of mind. And you know what's coming. Yeah, and you jam Trump in taxes. Yeah, I think that's what the Trump tax is all about. Another technique is what I call the magic wand technique. And if you could just pretend you could wave a magic wand and give the prospect exactly the solution they truly desire at a primal level, what would it look like? And then don't even think about when you start how to actually make it happen. But there's a promo I like to show people. It's kind of what not to do. It's called Lotto Pressure. And you may have seen it like, I haven't seen it in a while actually, but the practice is in the term jail. That's what I'm just going to say. Right, welcome. The promise was that using this Lotto Pressure technique, you could win the lottery whenever you wanted. I mean, and that definitely, if you wave the magic wand for an average person, that would be very appealing. Now, again, that's kind of what not to do because I don't really think they were able to fulfill that promise. Was that the guy on camera? It's a great sales. Guarantee, fuel, okay. That was the binary guy. No, similar to that though. This one, I'm going to go on a quick little tangent just because I think it's a really interesting series of promos. That Lotto Pressure thing has probably been written like four or five times over by different people, by different companies, which is why you know it's fake. But it always has to do with a guy who wins the lottery so much that he either gets kidnapped and basically held for like torture reasons to get the secret out of him or he gets shot in the parking lot. And like there's apparently eight different people in America that have this exact same story happen to them because I think what happens is the first person gets locked up but they make a lot of money and the second person says, I'll do that too. And then they go right basically the same promotion. But it's like, hey, I won the lottery seven times and I walked out on my seventh time of winning the lottery and these people shot me in the leg, put me in the back of their car and then tortured me to get the secret out of me. Somehow he survives, I can't remember. And now he's going to share the secret with you. You're saying these guys won the jump? I'm pretty sure they did. I don't think that story is real. And then I think I tried to order once and I got this like, because I'm like, okay, I'll see what they deliver. And then I tried to order and I hit the order button, you know, so I put in my credit card and I'm like, oh God. I kind of feel weird putting my credit card because I know these guys are scammers and I hit the order button and it just goes to a screen like page can't be found. I'm like, oh shit, like that's definitely going to be fucking Russia somewhere now. I was like, I'm going to call Visa and be like proactively. No, I just got hacked. How did you know you got hacked? Well, I'm just telling you I got hacked. Well, it's a shitty shit. I'll bring the transcript of the lead for that because I got a transcript of the BSL because I think the BSL is gone. And I'll bring it next week, the first page of the sales letter because it's a great sales letter. I promise I had a very good business idea. But that's, you know, like wave the magic wand. What do people want from the stock market? They want to know exactly when to buy the stock when it'll go up and exactly when to sell for the most profit. You can look at that for our man on the street income on demand promo where you wave magic wand, you get money out of a computer, you don't need anything, you don't even touch the stock market. So that's a way to work backwards and then figure out, well, how do we realistically back up that promise? And I'm just going to read off like 11 other ideas to get ideas before, okay, just to kind of warm you guys up because this is going to be a little exercise after this. But here are 11 other ideas to get ideas. Number one, reading our editorial slash products every day. You guys should definitely be reading the five minute forecast, the other reckoning. Maybe so much material you put out, you know, I don't read it everything every day, but you should be reading some of it every day. Maybe just focus on a particular product for a week or something and say, I'm only going to read currency worth alert this week and read every issue I can. You should be reading outside editorial books, Wall Street Journal, Zero Hedge, Rudge Report every day. You need to be speaking with our editors and our publishers, taking them out to lunch, whatever you get a chance to meet them at a holiday party, cornering them with party and just peppering them questions. Our publishers can hook you up with, you know, how to make the introduction if you don't know our editors or how to get a hold of them. But they're usually very open and we'll talk to you. So keep lists of ideas. I wrote down like stockpile ideas like a doomsday prepper. I mean, you want to have just piles and piles of ideas to go through. If you're down to only like a couple of ideas, you know, that's just like being down to your last couple of bullets, you know, and the zombie uprising. So write one new idea a day, which we're doing. Consume popular trash, TV, movies, best sellers, novels, tabloids. What is our market reading? What is our average reader reading beyond our stuff? I mean, it's probably not as sophisticated as we might assume sometimes. The index card method, which is where you just try to capture as many different ideas, proof elements, stories, quirky language, power words, get a whole stack of index cards, capturing everything you can think of, and then just like shuffle through them like cards and mix and match and say, this is connect. This is connect. Constantly pitching and sharing your ideas with other people inside our business. Always be pitching. Whenever you go get coffee or you're running into somebody going to the bathroom, you're pitching my ideas. What's that to say? AVP. Yeah, AVP. Always be pitching. Interacting and pitching people not in our business. If you ever have like a long Uber drive or you're taking a cab to the airport, talk to that guy and just see, you know, throw him some of your ideas and see what he says back. I love talking with Uber drivers and taxi cab drivers. They usually have a lot of insights, but it can be also family members, you know, especially closer to our target demographic. So if you have an uncle or a grandfather or your dad or something, talk to them. I mean, Scott Wright-Elly had an awesome piece of proof for a financial security. What he got from his Nana, which was Scrammer, where she really was using a variation of what he was using, the buying blue chip stocks and compounding dividends to make a lot of money. So he was able to interview his grandma for a key piece of proof. He also got, if you look at Scott's promo, he got his mother, the tax return in the Canadian Social Security thing about Ann Moynihan making $4,700 a year, is Scott's mom. That's her tax return in there about dividends that came out of her account based on the same stocks that were in the Canadian pension plan. So he wouldn't have gotten that unless he was talking to his family. Looking at the Amazon bestseller list or going to bookstores is a good way to maybe find an idea. Looking at the covers, like what's on the New York Times bestseller right now? That's going to give us a good sense of what people are interested in. The last idea I have is like how can you turn your ideas and thoughts into visual pictures, which means mind maps, flow charts, diagrams, and try to draw out, like you're trying to come up with a system for a system like promo, like draw a diagram of this needs to happen and this needs to happen and therefore you make money. Some of those things can be recycled as like proof in, you know, if you want a picture or something in your promo. But I think sometimes it's just a different way, if you're a more visual person too, but not everyone's a word thinker to have a visual of what you're thinking about. So I, okay, now it's... Can I go before you get there? Yeah. I do two random things. Sure. I think as we have been telling you guys, right, this is an experiment for AF Copy Team 2 and the idea is that if it goes well we're going to do AF Copy Team 3 at some point and we fill this room with 20 more people, we're all looking to do the same thing. So part of this is a refinement process. So I'm officially, next time, going to change the name of this one to Idea Sex and Zombies. This doesn't protect me for producing ideas. So it'll be session 4 number 4. Next thing is, hit this link real quick, either now or when you guys, you know, get back to your computer. It's bit.do backslash norman s or norman s. It is personally one of my favorite quotes and I'm going to tell you guys a quote real quick for those of you that can't see it and it says, the more you sweat in peace, the less you bleed in war. And it was by Norman Schwartzkopf. I think it's one of the most profound statements for, particularly all of my life, right, no matter if it's physical stuff I want to do, a challenge stuff I want to do, work and everything like that. So what does that mean? The more you sweat in peace, the less you bleed in war. The way I apply that to work is, idea generation is the most important and the hardest part. You need to sweat to generate ideas. If you do that, the easier copywriting will always be. So if you read more than anybody else you know, if you have, you know, if you stare off in space and think more than anybody else you know and let that idea sex happen in that super strange way in your head, if you sweat, it will make the copywriting part the easiest part when it's time to write copies. If you don't, then it makes copywriting extremely painful because you'll realize that you don't have an idea. You'll sit down and try to write 30 pages. You don't even know what your idea is. That's where you're going to bleed in war, if that makes sense, right? So it could be applied to like you run a marathon, right? The less, the more you sweat in peace the less you bleed in war. If you train for a marathon really, really, really hard, it's going to make the marathon easier to run. If you don't train at all and you strap on your shoes and you go try to run, you know, a marathon, it's going to hurt really bad, right? It's going to hurt when you try to write a promo and you do not have an idea because you haven't read anything. You don't know what's top of mind. It's that process of going through an idea every single day and reading a book every single week and reading a promotion every single day. There are 99% of the people in our business that are never going to do those things and they're never going to succeed as a copywriter. The 1% that's willing to work harder than everybody else that's willing to sweat from a mental side harder than anybody else and more than anybody else are going to be the people that succeed. So I like to quote, I think it's very applicable to what we do. Think about that. We're trying to go through this exercise altogether to say, this is why an idea in a day is important. Lindsay and I were talking about this yesterday and she goes, I can't remember how you phrased it because you said something like, how do I know I have a good idea? I was saying, well, 99% of my ideas are just terrible, but I have to go through those 99 ideas to try to get the one that is actually useful. So that's the sweating that we all do. Anyway, back to the caca. So a little sweat's going to go on now. I have 10 minutes. Can we come up with, and just raise your hand as you think of them, 10 other ideas to get ideas? I got one. Because they spend 30 minutes to an hour even looking at one idea perhaps. So similar to TED Talks, less production value so that you can get a lot more flow from those. He was actually put into the Nazi cabinet, Hitler's cabinet, and not killed. That's very strange, right? He was a known Jew who didn't get killed as part of Hitler's inner circle. So why is that? Why did a Jew, did he find a cure for cancer? Because that's what a ketogenic diet will start cancer cells. Did you say cancer cells earlier too? It is actually a way, if you look at like just the most, there's like a prop approach to ideas, which is you take a mainstream idea and you just try to force yourself to just prove the opposite. It's the mainstream saying like Hillary Clinton's going to get elected. That's an obvious one, right? So it's like, no, you can't be a contrarian for one of you. That's one way. I think it's important to like, like kind of, I think it's step three, kind of like not actively thinking about it. So like whether it's picking up a musical instrument or like picking up a hobby that you can like get lost in. I guess like super important. Yeah, that was in the books. I don't want to go too much. But like taking a shower, taking walks, playing golf. You got, I don't want to, you guys think you have to constantly be thinking all the time either or constantly be trying to come up with ideas because you do need to build in those breaks in your life because that's usually when the idea connects. So if you're 24 seven, I got to find the idea. That's probably not the best way to do it. You should build in and maybe intentionally like a couple hours where you just do nothing and that's out because chances are that's when it is going to pop in your head. Who read that? Did you guys all read the Steve Jobs book? Who read that? It's a great book. Steve Jobs took almost all of his meetings. I think he would just walk around in the California offices and he got a lot of his ideas just by walking around too. So it's kind of like that thing of forgetting and he would just literally walk around versus being in the building and he said a lot of his ideas came to him. When there's walks and he would have a lot of his meetings, he wouldn't take a meeting that would be like, oh, let's go sit in a conference room and that's going to be like, let's go take a walk. And you're not from left, right, right, right sense. I mean, you're not ever going to have a creative idea of just sitting there and actually doing stuff or booking numbers or whatever. That's the frustrating part. Yeah, I mean, I look at it, I work, I've worked a lot. I'll be doing the day, but I do a lot of the year at night. And it's like, day times when I get on the research desk, night times when I make a cool chair. That's having those expectations going in. I don't know if you were frustrated or it's like, you're waiting for the idea to come and it's not coming. Well, then you got to go and hit the books. But why are you doing that? You probably still like my comments coming afterwards. You got to make sure you capture that too. I email myself notes, like, I don't know if you guys ever saw that like, like, sign-file episode where he's finding like the napkin that he wrote some joke on and the whole upswit doesn't know what it is. And he finds out at the end it's something really stupid. A lot of my emails in the morning are just like that. I'm like, was I drunk? I don't even know what this means. But somehow capture those ideas because if you're trying to remember them later, sometimes you can lose them. So, you know, keep a notepad handy or email yourself. Find some way to collect those ideas because that could be gold and or it could be crap. But if you don't, if you're like, oh, I got to remember this, you know, and to drive home, chances are you're going to get home and be like, shit, like, I don't remember. I had a great idea, but it's gone. So try to capture them as instantaneously as possible. Okay, so next session, which is on Tuesday, we, this is going to conclude the idea section. Even though, again, just beat the dead horse completely dead, the most important thing is this. That's why we have spent three weeks on it. That's why we're trying to show everybody how to reverse engineer promos. That's why we, you know, Ryan worked hard to put a whole huge reading list together and why Pete spoke up so much about all the sources that he reads. This is the most important process. This is the idea like we talked about last time, which is if you give me five hours to chop down the tree, I'll spend the first four and a half sharpening the axe. This is you sharpening the axe. The idea is the most important. Next week, we're going to talk about how you start that idea. It's called a pattern interrupt. We're going to talk about the headline and we're going to talk about the lead once you have an idea. So that's going to be next week. So, again, keep writing down an idea day. Begin to think about when you start to look over all of your ideas, what idea do you want to write at the end of this thing when we get to Hell Week? Begin to think about narrowing down your ideas now. You don't have to pick one. But when you think about this, you're going to start to think about, you know, how do I interrupt someone's day with a pattern interrupt and a lead? So as we go through this, you might want to start to think about some ideas that you eventually want to turn into copy at the end of this thing. So you can start to put them through these exercises of lead and pattern interrupts and things like that. The other thing is just as a kind of reminder here so it doesn't approach everybody too fast, between sessions eight and nine, we're doing pitch week. So pitch week is when you're going to stand up here and you're going to have three minutes and you're going to pitch for three minutes your favorite idea that you have at that moment, that you want to write during Hell Week. So at that point during sessions eight and nine, it's going to be the first week of February, it's in your packets if you want to look at the actual dates, you should have 30 ideas down. You should have 30, 30 ideas down. You're going to pitch your favorite one in front of the group. We're probably going to do it like America's Got Talent style, where we're going to get big red X's. And I think Doug, Brian and I are going to sit in front of you guys and your idea is to get out of here without all three people Xing your idea. So I got to find these. They're very tough to find right now. I'm going to hit a big button and it's going to be like, 10 seconds into your idea. If you didn't do a pattern in a rough, you didn't do a lead, right? So you don't want to get X's. You got these at the same store. The big red light up X's are not as easy to find. But begin thinking about that because that's coming up in a couple weeks, right? Roughly two weeks or so. Again, you should have 30 ideas. Pick your favorite one. That's what you'll be pitching in a couple weeks. So next week, pattern interrupts, headlines, leads, that type of thing. Just know that in two more weeks it's coming up on idea pitching in front of the group. Should we read? Nobody wants to read your shirt. No. There's nothing, no reading aside that you don't have anything, right? Right next week. Just keep reading a promo today. Reading is coming at some point here when we get to influence techniques and things like that and making them believe. So you'll have a quote on quote assignment coming up in like two weeks or a week and a half, but not for next week. Yeah, it's not the book. It's just the idea though that nobody wants to read your shit. That's what we want to keep in mind as we talk about a pattern interrupt. So we'll talk about that. Know that your idea pitch week is coming. Three minutes in front of the group. Just let you guys know. If you put that down on paper, three minutes is about a page and a half. You get a page and a half that you're going to pitch to the group. If you read a page and a half out loud, depending on how fast you read, that's about three minutes. So know that you have a page and a half to pitch your idea verbally in front of the group in a couple weeks. So just know that that's coming. I don't want it to be a surprise to anybody. Everybody's doing it. It's going to be uncomfortable. Most copywriters are introverts. Most copywriters don't like, like I'm part of that, right? I don't like to get out there and do this type of shit. It's uncomfortable for me. But you got to know that pitching your idea is one of the most important things. So that's why we're doing it in a couple weeks. We can pattern interrupts next week. See you guys on Tuesday. Thank you.
